title: The night market that disappears at dawn
summary: Describe a market, fair, or temporary gathering that exists for only a few hours and reflect on why temporary places matter to a city.
topic: A market or gathering that exists for only a few hours
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing a market, night fair, or temporary gathering you have visited, and reflect on what cities lose when these temporary places disappear. Use the present perfect for ongoing observation, the past continuous for scene-setting, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: By half past five in the morning the market on Charoenkrung Road was beginning to dissolve, and what had been a city of stalls four hours earlier was…
If you had asked me a year ago whether a temporary market really counted as part of a city, I would have said…
Although guidebooks describe these places as colourful and chaotic, what is striking about them up close is something more orderly that I have only slowly learned to see…

hintVocab.meaningVi: nền kinh tế phi chính thức
những người đi làm đầu tiên

sampleText:
By half past five in the morning the market on Charoenkrung Road in Bangkok was already beginning to dissolve. What had been a small city of stalls four hours earlier — sixty or seventy tables under blue tarpaulins, a row of fryers in metal frames, three women selling jasmine garlands from low stools — was folding back into wheelbarrows, motorbike trailers, and plastic crates. Two of the vendors were sweeping their patch of pavement with stiff brooms. A man in his fifties, who had been frying flat noodles since just past midnight, was wiping down a wok the size of a small wheel. By six, when the first office commuters began crossing the street toward the train station, almost no trace of the night would be left. The fluorescent bulbs that had hung from string lines all night were already packed into a cardboard box on the back of a moped.

Although guidebooks describe these markets as colourful and chaotic, what struck me on the third or fourth visit was how orderly the whole thing was beneath the noise. The same vendors took the same spots every night. The same older women came in at the same hour to buy the leftover greens at a discount. The municipal workers who arrived at dawn knew which corners would need cleaning and which would be already swept. Nothing about the market was written down, but everyone involved had a clear sense of where they belonged and what they had to do by when. The market had been operating in this same shape, my friend later told me, for nearly thirty years, and the rhythm had been passed from one generation of stall-holders to the next through nothing more formal than habit.

What I have come to think, after watching markets like this in several cities, is that temporary places do something for a city that permanent buildings cannot. They give ordinary people a low-cost way to enter public life — a few hundred baht for a folding table, no rent, no permit application longer than a single page. They turn a piece of pavement that is empty most of the day into a space where people meet, eat, and recognise each other. However, this kind of arrangement is fragile. The moment a city decides to formalise the market — to charge real fees, to require licences, to clean it up for tourists — the original logic begins to break, and many of the older vendors stop coming. A market that disappears at dawn but reappears every night is doing something quietly important, and a city that loses these spaces does not always notice what it has lost until the pavement stays empty.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past continuous and past perfect together for scene-setting
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'A man in his fifties, who had been frying flat noodles since just past midnight, was wiping down a wok' kết hợp quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn ('had been frying') để mô tả hoạt động đã diễn ra trong một khoảng thời gian trước đó, và quá khứ tiếp diễn ('was wiping') để dựng cảnh tại thời điểm chính. Kỹ thuật này tạo chiều sâu thời gian cho một câu chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'A man in his fifties, who had been frying flat noodles since just past midnight, was wiping down a wok' combines the past perfect continuous ('had been frying') for an activity that had been going on for some time, and the past continuous ('was wiping') to set the scene at the main moment. This technique gives temporal depth to a narrative.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Reduced relative clauses to compress description
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'sixty or seventy tables under blue tarpaulins' và 'three women selling jasmine garlands from low stools' dùng mệnh đề quan hệ rút gọn — bỏ 'which were' và 'who were' — để dồn nhiều chi tiết vào một câu mà không lặp lại. Đây là kỹ thuật miêu tả quan trọng ở trình độ B1.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'sixty or seventy tables under blue tarpaulins' and 'three women selling jasmine garlands from low stools' use reduced relative clauses — dropping 'which were' and 'who were' — to pack many details into one sentence without repetition. This is a key descriptive technique at B1.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đến năm rưỡi sáng, khu chợ trên đường Charoenkrung ở Bangkok đã bắt đầu tan ra. Cái mà trước đó bốn giờ đã là một thành phố nhỏ của các quầy hàng — sáu bảy mươi cái bàn dưới những tấm bạt xanh, một dãy chảo chiên trong khung kim loại, ba người phụ nữ bán vòng hoa nhài từ những chiếc ghế đẩu thấp — đang gấp lại vào xe đẩy, móoc xe máy, và thùng nhựa. Hai người bán đang quét vỉa hè phần của họ bằng chổi cứng. Một người đàn ông ngoài năm mươi, người đã chiên mì dẹt từ ngay sau nửa đêm, đang lau một cái chảo to bằng nửa bánh xe. Đến sáu giờ, khi những người đi làm văn phòng đầu tiên bắt đầu băng qua đường đến nhà ga xe lửa, gần như không còn dấu vết nào của đêm. Những bóng đèn huỳnh quang đã treo từ dây thừng suốt đêm đã được xếp vào một hộp các-tông trên đuôi xe máy.

Mặc dù các cuốn sách hướng dẫn miêu tả những khu chợ này là đầy màu sắc và hỗn loạn, điều khiến tôi ấn tượng vào lần ghé thứ ba hay thứ tư là toàn bộ sự việc trật tự đến mức nào bên dưới sự ồn ào. Cùng những người bán hàng đó chiếm cùng những chỗ đó mỗi đêm. Cùng những bà cụ đến vào cùng giờ để mua rau thừa với giá giảm. Các nhân viên thành phố đến vào rạng đông biết những góc nào sẽ cần quét dọn và những góc nào đã được quét. Không gì về khu chợ được ghi lại bằng văn bản, nhưng mọi người tham gia đều có cảm giác rõ ràng về việc mình thuộc về đâu và phải làm gì vào khi nào. Khu chợ đã hoạt động theo cùng hình thức này, bạn tôi sau đó nói với tôi, gần ba mươi năm, và nhịp điệu đã được truyền từ thế hệ người bán hàng này sang thế hệ tiếp theo qua không gì chính thức hơn ngoài thói quen.

Điều tôi dần nghĩ, sau khi quan sát những khu chợ như thế này ở vài thành phố, là những nơi tạm thời làm điều gì đó cho thành phố mà các tòa nhà cố định không thể. Chúng cho người bình thường một cách chi phí thấp để bước vào đời sống công cộng — vài trăm baht cho một cái bàn xếp, không tiền thuê, không đơn xin phép dài hơn một trang. Chúng biến một mảnh vỉa hè trống vắng phần lớn ngày thành một không gian nơi người ta gặp nhau, ăn, và nhận ra nhau. Tuy nhiên, kiểu thoả thuận này mong manh. Khoảnh khắc một thành phố quyết định chính thức hoá khu chợ — thu phí thực, yêu cầu giấy phép, dọn sạch nó cho khách du lịch — logic ban đầu bắt đầu sụp đổ, và nhiều người bán hàng lớn tuổi ngừng đến. Một khu chợ biến mất vào rạng đông nhưng xuất hiện lại mỗi đêm đang làm điều gì đó âm thầm quan trọng, và một thành phố mất những không gian này không phải lúc nào cũng nhận ra điều nó đã mất cho đến khi vỉa hè vẫn trống.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect continuous?
options: He had been frying flat noodles since just past midnight.
He had being frying flat noodles since just past midnight.
He was been frying flat noodles since just past midnight.
He has been frying flat noodles since just past midnight.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'; describes an action that had been ongoing up to a past reference point.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past continuous correctly to set a scene?
options: Two of the vendors was sweeping their patch of pavement with stiff brooms.
Two of the vendors were sweeping their patch of pavement with stiff brooms.
Two of the vendors were sweep their patch of pavement with stiff brooms.
Two of the vendors were swept their patch of pavement with stiff brooms.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past continuous = 'was/were + -ing'. The subject 'two of the vendors' is plural and takes 'were'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a reduced relative clause correctly?
options: Three women they selling jasmine garlands from low stools.
Three women who selling jasmine garlands from low stools.
Three women selling jasmine garlands from low stools.
Three women whom selling jasmine garlands from low stools.
mcQuestions.explanation: A reduced relative clause drops 'who were' and uses the bare -ing form. Adding 'who selling' or 'whom selling' creates an incorrect mixed structure.
mcQuestions.hint: Reduced relative clause: drop 'who were' and use the bare -ing form.

options: Although guidebooks describe these markets as chaotic, but what struck me was how orderly the whole thing was.
Although, guidebooks describe these markets as chaotic what struck me was how orderly the whole thing was.
Guidebooks describe these markets as chaotic, although however what struck me was how orderly the whole thing was.
Although guidebooks describe these markets as chaotic, what struck me was how orderly the whole thing was.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling with 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'although' alone; do not add 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly?
options: What I have come to think is that temporary places do something for a city.
What I have came to think is that temporary places do something for a city.
What I came to thinking is that temporary places do something for a city.
What I have come thinking is that temporary places do something for a city.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect 'have come' + 'to + base verb'. Past participle of 'come' is 'come'; 'to think', not 'thinking'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have come to + base verb' for gradual realisations.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the past perfect correctly?
options: What has been a small city of stalls four hours earlier was folding back into wheelbarrows.
What had been a small city of stalls four hours earlier was folding back into wheelbarrows.
What had be a small city of stalls four hours earlier was folding back into wheelbarrows.
What had being a small city of stalls four hours earlier was folding back into wheelbarrows.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; 'been' is the past participle of 'be'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'the moment' as a conjunction correctly?
options: The moment that when a city decides to formalise the market, the original logic begins to break.
The moment a city will decide to formalise the market, the original logic begins to break.
The moment a city decides to formalise the market, the original logic begins to break.
The moment a city decide to formalise the market, the original logic begins to break.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The moment + present clause' means 'as soon as'. No 'will' is needed in a time clause; third-person singular agreement ('decides').
mcQuestions.hint: Time clauses use the present, not the future; ensure third-person agreement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'permanent buildings cannot' correctly to close a clause?
options: Temporary places do something for a city that permanent buildings cannot to.
Temporary places do something for a city that permanent buildings cannot do it.
Temporary places do something for a city that permanent buildings cannot does.
Temporary places do something for a city that permanent buildings cannot.
mcQuestions.explanation: Ellipsis after a modal: the modal 'cannot' alone implies the full verb phrase from before ('do something for a city'). Adding 'to', 'it', or 'does' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: After a modal, the verb phrase can be left implied (ellipsis); no extra words are added.

options: By six, almost no trace of the night would be left.
Until six, almost no trace of the night would be left.
By six, almost no trace of the night would left.
Until six, almost no trace of the night would left.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'By + time' means 'no later than that time'. 'Until' would mean 'up to that time and continuing'. Passive needs 'be + past participle'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'By + time' = no later than; passive needs 'be + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'recognise each other' correctly?
options: A space where people meet, eat, and recognise themselves.
A space where people meet, eat, and recognise each other.
A space where people meet, eat, and recognise.
A space where people meet, eat, and recognise other each.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Each other' is the reciprocal pronoun (people recognise different people); 'themselves' would mean each person recognises themselves alone. Word order is fixed.
mcQuestions.hint: Reciprocal action between people uses 'each other', not 'themselves'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'not always' correctly in mid-position?
options: A city that loses these spaces does always not notice what it has lost.
A city that loses these spaces not always does notice what it has lost.
A city that loses these spaces does not always notice what it has lost.
A city that loses these spaces does not notice always what it has lost.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not always' typically goes between the auxiliary ('does') and the main verb ('notice'); 'not' precedes 'always' in this collocation.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If formalising a market often destroys the very thing that made it valuable, is there any way for a city government to support these spaces without absorbing them — and what would that look like?
